agent_output: {"summary":{"verdict":"SHIP WITH FIXES","one_liner":"Off-by-one pagination bug: page 1 skips the first pageSize rows because offset calc assumes 0-indexed pages despite API contract being 1-indexed."},"critical_findings":[{"title":"Off-by-one: 1-indexed page contract vs 0-indexed offset calculation","risk":"Page 1 returns the second page of results; page 0 (undocumented) returns the actual first page. Every consumer of this API sees wrong data.","severity":"Critical","location":"getOrderHistory, line: const offset = page * pageSize","reproduction":"Call getOrderHistory('cust_1', 1, 10). Expected: rows 0-9 (OFFSET 0). Actual: OFFSET 10, returns rows 10-19. The real first page is unreachable via documented API.","fix":"Change offset calculation to: const offset = (page - 1) * pageSize;","time_to_fix":"< 5 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"API documentation states page=1 is the first page; current implementation violates this contract."},{"title":"No input validation allows SQL abuse and negative offsets","risk":"page=0 or negative values produce OFFSET 0 or negative offsets (Postgres treats negative OFFSET as 0, but semantics are undefined in the API). pageSize=0 returns nothing; pageSize=-1 in Postgres returns all rows (equivalent to no LIMIT), enabling full-table dump DoS.","severity":"High","location":"getOrderHistory, parameters page and pageSize","reproduction":"Call getOrderHistory('cust_1', 1, -1). The query becomes LIMIT -1 which Postgres interprets as no limit, returning the entire orders table for that customer.","fix":"Add guards: if (page < 1) throw new Error('page must be >= 1'); if (pageSize < 1 || pageSize > 100) throw new Error('pageSize must be 1-100');","time_to_fix":"5 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"API safety and rate limiting expectations."},{"title":"No total count returned — clients cannot build pagination UI","risk":"Without a total count or hasNextPage indicator, consumers have no way to know when to stop paginating. They must guess or paginate until an empty array is returned, causing unnecessary requests.","severity":"High","location":"getOrderHistory return type Promise<Order[]>","reproduction":"Client calls page=1, gets 10 results. Are there more? Unknown. Client must call page=2 and check if empty — an extra round trip on the last page every time.","fix":"Return { orders: Order[], total: number } by adding a COUNT(*) query (or use a window function: COUNT(*) OVER() AS total_count) and update the return type.","time_to_fix":"15 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Pagination usability for any frontend or API consumer."}],"important_issues":[{"title":"SELECT * exposes all columns including potentially sensitive data","description":"SELECT * returns every column from the orders table. If columns like internal_notes, payment_token, or margin are added later, they leak to the client without review. Use an explicit column list.","location":"SQL query: SELECT * FROM orders"},{"title":"OFFSET-based pagination degrades at scale","description":"OFFSET N forces Postgres to scan and discard N rows before returning results. For customers with large order histories, high page numbers become progressively slower. Consider keyset/cursor pagination (WHERE created_at < $lastSeen) for production scale.","location":"SQL query: OFFSET $3"}],"suggestions":["Add an index on (customer_id, created_at DESC) if one does not exist — this query will full-scan without it.","Consider adding a stable tiebreaker to ORDER BY (e.g., ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC) to prevent row duplication/skipping when multiple orders share the same created_at timestamp.","Type the return as a pagination envelope: { data: Order[], page: number, pageSize: number, total: number }."],"production_readiness":{"deployment_risks":"The off-by-one bug means every page a user sees is wrong today. Fixing the offset changes which rows every existing page number returns — any client caching page results will see a shift.","rollback_capability":"Stateless query — rollback is trivial, just redeploy the previous version.","backward_compatibility":"If any client has been compensating for the bug by sending page=0 for the first page, the fix will break them. Verify no client workarounds exist before deploying.","monitoring_coverage":"No mention of logging or metrics. Add latency tracking per query and alert on high OFFSET values.","breaking_changes":{"api_breaking":true,"db_destructive":false,"shared_library":false,"auth_security":false,"data_pipeline":false,"config_changes":false,"details":["Fixing the offset changes the result set for every page number — technically a breaking change for any consumer relying on current (buggy) behavior."]},"safe_to_deploy":false,"rollback_plan":"Revert the single function change; no database migration involved."},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":95,"high_confidence_areas":["Off-by-one bug is deterministic and trivially reproducible","Input validation gap is confirmed by reading Postgres LIMIT semantics"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether any downstream client has already worked around the bug by using page=0"],"assumptions":["Database is PostgreSQL based on $1/$2/$3 parameterized query syntax","No middleware validation exists upstream of this function"]}}
